displayName: Detect configuration settings where config value differs from run value â€” RECONFIGURE not executed after sp_configure

expected_result: No config vs run value mismatches â€” all changes applied via RECONFIGURE
condition: SELECT [name] AS unsaved_option FROM ( SELECT [name], CASE WHEN configuration_id = 1543 AND [value] = 0 AND value_in_use <> 0 THEN CASE WHEN CAST(SERVERPROPERTY('Edition') AS NVARCHAR(128)) LIKE '%(64-bit)' THEN 16 ELSE 8 END WHEN configuration_id = 1544 AND [value] = 0 AND value_in_use <> 0 THEN 2147483647 ELSE [value] END AS configured_value, value_in_use FROM sys.configurations ) AS i WHERE configured_value <> value_in_use

displayName: Detect tables with zero indexes â€” pure heaps with no nonclustered indexes are inefficient for queries
description: Tables without any indexes (not even nonclustered) are pure heaps with no optimization for queries. All data access requires full table scans. Even small tables benefit from at least one index for common query patterns.
message: Review: Detect tables with zero indexes â€” pure heaps with no nonclustered indexes are inefficient for queries
sql_query: SELECT s.name + '.' + t.name AS TableName FROM sys.indexes AS si (NOLOCK) INNER JOIN sys.tables AS t (NOLOCK) ON si.[object_id] = t.[object_id] INNER JOIN sys.schemas AS s (NOLOCK) ON s.[schema_id] = t.[schema_id] WHERE si.is_hypothetical = 0 AND OBJECTPROPERTY(t.[object_id], 'IsMSShipped') = 0 GROUP BY s.name, t.name HAVING COUNT(si.index_id) = 0
expected_result: No user tables without any indexes
condition: SELECT s.name + '.' + t.name AS TableName FROM sys.indexes AS si (NOLOCK) INNER JOIN sys.tables AS t (NOLOCK) ON si.[object_id] = t.[object_id] INNER JOIN sys.schemas AS s (NOLOCK) ON s.[schema_id] = t.[schema_id] WHERE si.is_hypothetical = 0 AND OBJECTPROPERTY(t.[object_id], 'IsMSShipped') = 0 GROUP BY s.name, t.name HAVING COUNT(si.index_id) = 0

displayName: Detect tables without clustered indexes (heaps) â€” scattered storage degrades scan, update, and delete performance
description: Tables without clustered indexes are called heaps. Data is scattered on disk with no physical ordering. This makes inserts fast but selects, updates, and deletes slow. Heaps also cannot benefit from ordered index seeks and cause forwarding pointer overhead on variable-length column updates.
message: Review: Detect tables without clustered indexes (heaps) â€” scattered storage degrades scan, update, and delete performance
sql_query: SELECT s.name + '.' + t.name AS TableName, COUNT(si.index_id) AS IndexCount FROM sys.indexes AS si (NOLOCK) INNER JOIN sys.tables AS t (NOLOCK) ON si.[object_id] = t.[object_id] INNER JOIN sys.schemas AS s (NOLOCK) ON s.[schema_id] = t.[schema_id] WHERE si.is_hypothetical = 0 AND OBJECTPROPERTY(t.[object_id], 'IsMSShipped') = 0 AND si.type = 0 GROUP BY s.name, t.name
expected_result: No user tables without clustered indexes (except documented heaps)
condition: SELECT s.name + '.' + t.name AS TableName, COUNT(si.index_id) AS IndexCount FROM sys.indexes AS si (NOLOCK) INNER JOIN sys.tables AS t (NOLOCK) ON si.[object_id] = t.[object_id] INNER JOIN sys.schemas AS s (NOLOCK) ON s.[schema_id] = t.[schema_id] WHERE si.is_hypothetical = 0 AND OBJECTPROPERTY(t.[object_id], 'IsMSShipped') = 0 AND si.type = 0 GROUP BY s.name, t.name

displayName: Create indexes on foreign key columns â€” FK constraints without supporting indexes degrade JOIN and DELETE performance
description: Unlike primary key constraints, creating a foreign key constraint does not automatically create a corresponding index. Without supporting indexes, DELETE operations on the parent table require scans of the child table to verify referential integrity, and JOIN operations on FK columns cannot use index seeks.
message: Review: Create indexes on foreign key columns â€” FK constraints without supporting indexes degrade JOIN and DELETE performance
sql_query: SELECT QUOTENAME(OBJECT_SCHEMA_NAME(FKC.parent_object_id)) + '.' + QUOTENAME(OBJECT_NAME(FKC.parent_object_id)) AS TableName, QUOTENAME(FKC.name) AS FKName FROM sys.foreign_keys FKC (NOLOCK) INNER JOIN sys.objects o (NOLOCK) ON FKC.parent_object_id = o.[object_id] WHERE o.type = 'U' AND o.is_ms_shipped = 0 AND NOT EXISTS ( SELECT 1 FROM sys.indexes i (NOLOCK) INNER JOIN sys.index_columns ic (NOLOCK) ON i.[object_id] = ic.[object_id] AND i.[index_id] = ic.[index_id] WHERE i.[object_id] = FKC.parent_object_id AND ic.column_id IN ( SELECT parent_column_id FROM sys.foreign_key_columns fkic (NOLOCK) WHERE fkic.constraint_object_id = FKC.[object_id] ) GROUP BY i.index_id HAVING COUNT(*) = ( SELECT COUNT(*) FROM sys.foreign_key_columns fkic (NOLOCK) WHERE fkic.constraint_object_id = FKC.[object_id] ) )
expected_result: All foreign key columns have supporting indexes
condition: SELECT QUOTENAME(OBJECT_SCHEMA_NAME(FKC.parent_object_id)) + '.' + QUOTENAME(OBJECT_NAME(FKC.parent_object_id)) AS TableName, QUOTENAME(FKC.name) AS FKName FROM sys.foreign_keys FKC (NOLOCK) INNER JOIN sys.objects o (NOLOCK) ON FKC.parent_object_id = o.[object_id] WHERE o.type = 'U' AND o.is_ms_shipped = 0 AND NOT EXISTS ( SELECT 1 FROM sys.indexes i (NOLOCK) INNER JOIN sys.index_columns ic (NOLOCK) ON i.[object_id] = ic.[object_id] AND i.[index_id] = ic.[index_id] WHERE i.[object_id] = FKC.parent_object_id AND ic.column_id IN ( SELECT parent_column_id FROM sys.foreign_key_columns fkic (NOLOCK) WHERE fkic.constraint_object_id = FKC.[object_id] ) GROUP BY i.index_id HAVING COUNT(*) = ( SELECT COUNT(*) FROM sys.foreign_key_columns fkic (NOLOCK) WHERE fkic.constraint_object_id = FKC.[object_id] ) )

displayName: Detect duplicate indexes (same key columns) â€” waste storage, memory, and write performance
description: Duplicate indexes have identical key column sets. Each duplicate must be maintained during INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE operations, doubling write overhead for no query benefit. The optimizer may also choose the wrong index, causing plan variability.
message: Review: Detect duplicate indexes (same key columns) â€” waste storage, memory, and write performance
sql_query: SELECT QUOTENAME(OBJECT_SCHEMA_NAME(i1.object_id)) + '.' + QUOTENAME(OBJECT_NAME(i1.object_id)) AS TableName, QUOTENAME(i1.name) AS Index1, QUOTENAME(i2.name) AS Index2 FROM sys.indexes i1 (NOLOCK) INNER JOIN sys.indexes i2 (NOLOCK) ON i1.object_id = i2.object_id AND i1.index_id < i2.index_id WHERE i1.type IN (1, 2) AND i2.type IN (1, 2) AND i1.is_primary_key = 0 AND i2.is_primary_key = 0 AND i1.is_unique_constraint = 0 AND i2.is_unique_constraint = 0 AND NOT EXISTS ( SELECT ic1.column_id, ic1.is_included_column EXCEPT SELECT ic2.column_id, ic2.is_included_column FROM sys.index_columns ic1 (NOLOCK) INNER JOIN sys.index_columns ic2 (NOLOCK) ON ic1.object_id = ic2.object_id AND ic1.index_id = i1.index_id AND ic2.index_id = i2.index_id AND ic1.column_id = ic2.column_id AND ic1.is_included_column = ic2.is_included_column WHERE ic1.object_id = i1.object_id )
expected_result: No duplicate indexes on user tables
condition: SELECT QUOTENAME(OBJECT_SCHEMA_NAME(i1.object_id)) + '.' + QUOTENAME(OBJECT_NAME(i1.object_id)) AS TableName, QUOTENAME(i1.name) AS Index1, QUOTENAME(i2.name) AS Index2 FROM sys.indexes i1 (NOLOCK) INNER JOIN sys.indexes i2 (NOLOCK) ON i1.object_id = i2.object_id AND i1.index_id < i2.index_id WHERE i1.type IN (1, 2) AND i2.type IN (1, 2) AND i1.is_primary_key = 0 AND i2.is_primary_key = 0 AND i1.is_unique_constraint = 0 AND i2.is_unique_constraint = 0 AND NOT EXISTS ( SELECT ic1.column_id, ic1.is_included_column EXCEPT SELECT ic2.column_id, ic2.is_included_column FROM sys.index_columns ic1 (NOLOCK) INNER JOIN sys.index_columns ic2 (NOLOCK) ON ic1.object_id = ic2.object_id AND ic1.index_id = i1.index_id AND ic2.index_id = i2.index_id AND ic1.column_id = ic2.column_id AND ic1.is_included_column = ic2.is_included_column WHERE ic1.object_id = i1.object_id )

displayName: Detect redundant indexes â€” one index's key columns are a subset of another's, providing no additional query benefit
description: Redundant indexes have key columns that are a proper subset of another index on the same table. The wider index can satisfy all queries that the narrower index serves, making the narrower index pure overhead for write operations.
message: Review: Detect redundant indexes â€” one index's key columns are a subset of another's, providing no additional query benefit
sql_query: SELECT QUOTENAME(OBJECT_SCHEMA_NAME(i1.object_id)) + '.' + QUOTENAME(OBJECT_NAME(i1.object_id)) AS TableName, QUOTENAME(i1.name) AS RedundantIndex, QUOTENAME(i2.name) AS CoveredByIndex FROM sys.indexes i1 (NOLOCK) INNER JOIN sys.indexes i2 (NOLOCK) ON i1.object_id = i2.object_id AND i1.index_id <> i2.index_id WHERE i1.type IN (1, 2) AND i2.type IN (1, 2) AND i1.is_primary_key = 0 AND i2.is_primary_key = 0 AND i1.is_unique_constraint = 0 AND i2.is_unique_constraint = 0 AND ( SELECT COUNT(*) FROM sys.index_columns ic1 (NOLOCK) WHERE ic1.object_id = i1.object_id AND ic1.index_id = i1.index_id AND ic1.is_included_column = 0 ) < ( SELECT COUNT(*) FROM sys.index_columns ic2 (NOLOCK) WHERE ic2.object_id = i2.object_id AND ic2.index_id = i2.index_id AND ic2.is_included_column = 0 ) AND NOT EXISTS ( SELECT ic1.column_id, ic1.key_ordinal FROM sys.index_columns ic1 (NOLOCK) WHERE ic1.object_id = i1.object_id AND ic1.index_id = i1.index_id AND ic1.is_included_column = 0 EXCEPT SELECT ic2.column_id, ic2.key_ordinal FROM sys.index_columns ic2 (NOLOCK) WHERE ic2.object_id = i2.object_id AND ic2.index_id = i2.index_id AND ic2.is_included_column = 0 AND ic2.key_ordinal <= ( SELECT MAX(key_ordinal) FROM sys.index_columns ic3 (NOLOCK) WHERE ic3.object_id = i1.object_id AND ic3.index_id = i1.index_id AND ic3.is_included_column = 0 ) )
expected_result: No redundant indexes on user tables
condition: SELECT QUOTENAME(OBJECT_SCHEMA_NAME(i1.object_id)) + '.' + QUOTENAME(OBJECT_NAME(i1.object_id)) AS TableName, QUOTENAME(i1.name) AS RedundantIndex, QUOTENAME(i2.name) AS CoveredByIndex FROM sys.indexes i1 (NOLOCK) INNER JOIN sys.indexes i2 (NOLOCK) ON i1.object_id = i2.object_id AND i1.index_id <> i2.index_id WHERE i1.type IN (1, 2) AND i2.type IN (1, 2) AND i1.is_primary_key = 0 AND i2.is_primary_key = 0 AND i1.is_unique_constraint = 0 AND i2.is_unique_constraint = 0 AND ( SELECT COUNT(*) FROM sys.index_columns ic1 (NOLOCK) WHERE ic1.object_id = i1.object_id AND ic1.index_id = i1.index_id AND ic1.is_included_column = 0 ) < ( SELECT COUNT(*) FROM sys.index_columns ic2 (NOLOCK) WHERE ic2.object_id = i2.object_id AND ic2.index_id = i2.index_id AND ic2.is_included_column = 0 ) AND NOT EXISTS ( SELECT ic1.column_id, ic1.key_ordinal FROM sys.index_columns ic1 (NOLOCK) WHERE ic1.object_id = i1.object_id AND ic1.index_id = i1.index_id AND ic1.is_included_column = 0 EXCEPT SELECT ic2.column_id, ic2.key_ordinal FROM sys.index_columns ic2 (NOLOCK) WHERE ic2.object_id = i2.object_id AND ic2.index_id = i2.index_id AND ic2.is_included_column = 0 AND ic2.key_ordinal <= ( SELECT MAX(key_ordinal) FROM sys.index_columns ic3 (NOLOCK) WHERE ic3.object_id = i1.object_id AND ic3.index_id = i1.index_id AND ic3.is_included_column = 0 ) )

displayName: Detect GUID (uniqueidentifier) in clustered index key â€” causes severe fragmentation due to random insert order
description: GUIDs are random values that cause new rows to be inserted at random positions in the clustered index, leading to page splits and severe fragmentation. Sequential GUIDs (NEWSEQUENTIALID()) partially mitigate this but are still suboptimal. Use identity columns or other sequential keys for clustered indexes.
message: Review: Detect GUID (uniqueidentifier) in clustered index key â€” causes severe fragmentation due to random insert order
sql_query: SELECT QUOTENAME(ss.[name]) + '.' + QUOTENAME(t.[name]) + '.' + QUOTENAME(i.[name]) AS IndexName FROM sys.indexes AS i WITH (NOLOCK) INNER JOIN sys.tables AS t WITH (NOLOCK) ON t.[object_id] = i.[object_id] INNER JOIN sys.schemas AS ss WITH (NOLOCK) ON ss.[schema_id] = t.[schema_id] INNER JOIN sys.index_columns AS sic WITH (NOLOCK) ON sic.[object_id] = t.[object_id] AND sic.index_id = i.index_id INNER JOIN sys.columns AS sc WITH (NOLOCK) ON sc.[object_id] = t.[object_id] AND sc.column_id = sic.column_id INNER JOIN sys.types AS sty WITH (NOLOCK) ON sc.user_type_id = sty.user_type_id WHERE sic.is_included_column = 0 AND sty.name = 'uniqueidentifier' AND i.type = 1 AND i.is_unique_constraint = 0 AND t.is_ms_shipped = 0
expected_result: No GUID columns in clustered index keys
condition: SELECT QUOTENAME(ss.[name]) + '.' + QUOTENAME(t.[name]) + '.' + QUOTENAME(i.[name]) AS IndexName FROM sys.indexes AS i WITH (NOLOCK) INNER JOIN sys.tables AS t WITH (NOLOCK) ON t.[object_id] = i.[object_id] INNER JOIN sys.schemas AS ss WITH (NOLOCK) ON ss.[schema_id] = t.[schema_id] INNER JOIN sys.index_columns AS sic WITH (NOLOCK) ON sic.[object_id] = t.[object_id] AND sic.index_id = i.index_id INNER JOIN sys.columns AS sc WITH (NOLOCK) ON sc.[object_id] = t.[object_id] AND sc.column_id = sic.column_id INNER JOIN sys.types AS sty WITH (NOLOCK) ON sc.user_type_id = sty.user_type_id WHERE sic.is_included_column = 0 AND sty.name = 'uniqueidentifier' AND i.type = 1 AND i.is_unique_constraint = 0 AND t.is_ms_shipped = 0

displayName: Detect non-unique clustered indexes â€” SQL Server adds a 4-byte uniqueifier overhead to every duplicate key
description: When a clustered index is not unique, SQL Server adds a 4-byte integer uniqueifier to every duplicate instance of the clustering key value. This uniqueifier is stored in both clustered and non-clustered indexes. With many duplicates, this overhead becomes significant across all indexes on the table.
message: Review: Detect non-unique clustered indexes â€” SQL Server adds a 4-byte uniqueifier overhead to every duplicate key
sql_query: SELECT QUOTENAME(OBJECT_SCHEMA_NAME(i.object_id)) + '.' + QUOTENAME(OBJECT_NAME(i.object_id)) + '.' + QUOTENAME(i.name) AS IndexName FROM sys.indexes i (NOLOCK) INNER JOIN sys.tables t (NOLOCK) ON t.[object_id] = i.[object_id] WHERE i.type = 1 AND i.is_unique = 0 AND i.is_primary_key = 0 AND i.is_unique_constraint = 0 AND t.is_ms_shipped = 0
expected_result: All clustered indexes are unique (or documented exceptions)
condition: SELECT QUOTENAME(OBJECT_SCHEMA_NAME(i.object_id)) + '.' + QUOTENAME(OBJECT_NAME(i.object_id)) + '.' + QUOTENAME(i.name) AS IndexName FROM sys.indexes i (NOLOCK) INNER JOIN sys.tables t (NOLOCK) ON t.[object_id] = i.[object_id] WHERE i.type = 1 AND i.is_unique = 0 AND i.is_primary_key = 0 AND i.is_unique_constraint = 0 AND t.is_ms_shipped = 0

displayName: Detect rarely used indexes â€” low read count with ongoing write overhead should be evaluated for removal
description: Indexes with very few reads compared to writes provide minimal query benefit while imposing continuous write overhead. Compare the read/write ratio to determine if the index cost justifies its benefit.
message: Review: Detect rarely used indexes â€” low read count with ongoing write overhead should be evaluated for removal
sql_query: SELECT QUOTENAME(OBJECT_SCHEMA_NAME(i.object_id)) + '.' + QUOTENAME(OBJECT_NAME(i.object_id)) AS TableName, QUOTENAME(i.name) AS IndexName, ISNULL(ius.user_seeks, 0) + ISNULL(ius.user_scans, 0) + ISNULL(ius.user_lookups, 0) AS TotalReads, ISNULL(ius.user_updates, 0) AS TotalWrites FROM sys.indexes i (NOLOCK) LEFT JOIN sys.dm_db_index_usage_stats ius ON i.object_id = ius.object_id AND i.index_id = ius.index_id AND ius.database_id = DB_ID() INNER JOIN sys.tables t (NOLOCK) ON i.object_id = t.object_id WHERE i.type IN (1, 2) AND i.is_primary_key = 0 AND i.is_unique_constraint = 0 AND t.is_ms_shipped = 0 AND ISNULL(ius.user_seeks, 0) + ISNULL(ius.user_scans, 0) + ISNULL(ius.user_lookups, 0) < 50 AND ISNULL(ius.user_updates, 0) > 1000
expected_result: Rarely used indexes reviewed and removed where appropriate
condition: SELECT QUOTENAME(OBJECT_SCHEMA_NAME(i.object_id)) + '.' + QUOTENAME(OBJECT_NAME(i.object_id)) AS TableName, QUOTENAME(i.name) AS IndexName, ISNULL(ius.user_seeks, 0) + ISNULL(ius.user_scans, 0) + ISNULL(ius.user_lookups, 0) AS TotalReads, ISNULL(ius.user_updates, 0) AS TotalWrites FROM sys.indexes i (NOLOCK) LEFT JOIN sys.dm_db_index_usage_stats ius ON i.object_id = ius.object_id AND i.index_id = ius.index_id AND ius.database_id = DB_ID() INNER JOIN sys.tables t (NOLOCK) ON i.object_id = t.object_id WHERE i.type IN (1, 2) AND i.is_primary_key = 0 AND i.is_unique_constraint = 0 AND t.is_ms_shipped = 0 AND ISNULL(ius.user_seeks, 0) + ISNULL(ius.user_scans, 0) + ISNULL(ius.user_lookups, 0) < 50 AND ISNULL(ius.user_updates, 0) > 1000

displayName: Detect nonclustered indexes with key columns exceeding 1700 bytes â€” approaching SQL Server maximum index key size
description: SQL Server has a 1700-byte limit for the maximum total size of all nonclustered index key columns (excluding included columns). Indexes approaching this limit may fail to create or cause unexpected behavior. Monitor key column sizes, especially with nvarchar columns (max_length is stored as character count * 2).
message: Review: Detect nonclustered indexes with key columns exceeding 1700 bytes â€” approaching SQL Server maximum index key size
sql_query: SELECT QUOTENAME(OBJECT_SCHEMA_NAME(i.object_id)) + '.' + QUOTENAME(OBJECT_NAME(i.object_id)) AS TableName, QUOTENAME(i.name) AS IndexName, SUM(CASE sty.name WHEN 'nvarchar' THEN sc.max_length / 2 ELSE sc.max_length END) AS TotalKeyBytes FROM sys.indexes i (NOLOCK) INNER JOIN sys.index_columns ic (NOLOCK) ON i.object_id = ic.object_id AND i.index_id = ic.index_id INNER JOIN sys.columns sc (NOLOCK) ON i.object_id = sc.object_id AND ic.column_id = sc.column_id INNER JOIN sys.types sty (NOLOCK) ON sc.user_type_id = sty.user_type_id INNER JOIN sys.tables t (NOLOCK) ON i.object_id = t.object_id WHERE i.type = 2 AND ic.is_included_column = 0 AND t.is_ms_shipped = 0 GROUP BY i.object_id, i.name HAVING SUM(CASE sty.name WHEN 'nvarchar' THEN sc.max_length / 2 ELSE sc.max_length END) > 1500
expected_result: No nonclustered indexes with key size exceeding 1500 bytes
condition: SELECT QUOTENAME(OBJECT_SCHEMA_NAME(i.object_id)) + '.' + QUOTENAME(OBJECT_NAME(i.object_id)) AS TableName, QUOTENAME(i.name) AS IndexName, SUM(CASE sty.name WHEN 'nvarchar' THEN sc.max_length / 2 ELSE sc.max_length END) AS TotalKeyBytes FROM sys.indexes i (NOLOCK) INNER JOIN sys.index_columns ic (NOLOCK) ON i.object_id = ic.object_id AND i.index_id = ic.index_id INNER JOIN sys.columns sc (NOLOCK) ON i.object_id = sc.object_id AND ic.column_id = sc.column_id INNER JOIN sys.types sty (NOLOCK) ON sc.user_type_id = sty.user_type_id INNER JOIN sys.tables t (NOLOCK) ON i.object_id = t.object_id WHERE i.type = 2 AND ic.is_included_column = 0 AND t.is_ms_shipped = 0 GROUP BY i.object_id, i.name HAVING SUM(CASE sty.name WHEN 'nvarchar' THEN sc.max_length / 2 ELSE sc.max_length END) > 1500

displayName: Detect disabled indexes â€” may indicate forgotten post-data-load rebuilds
description: Indexes are sometimes disabled before large data loads to improve insert performance. If the re-enable step is forgotten, queries lose the benefit of those indexes. Regularly check for disabled indexes and either rebuild or remove them.
message: Review: Detect disabled indexes â€” may indicate forgotten post-data-load rebuilds
sql_query: SELECT QUOTENAME(OBJECT_SCHEMA_NAME(i.object_id)) + '.' + QUOTENAME(OBJECT_NAME(i.object_id)) AS TableName, QUOTENAME(i.name) AS IndexName, i.type_desc FROM sys.indexes i (NOLOCK) INNER JOIN sys.tables t (NOLOCK) ON i.object_id = t.object_id WHERE i.is_disabled = 1 AND t.is_ms_shipped = 0
expected_result: No disabled indexes â€” all rebuilt or intentionally removed
condition: SELECT QUOTENAME(OBJECT_SCHEMA_NAME(i.object_id)) + '.' + QUOTENAME(OBJECT_NAME(i.object_id)) AS TableName, QUOTENAME(i.name) AS IndexName, i.type_desc FROM sys.indexes i (NOLOCK) INNER JOIN sys.tables t (NOLOCK) ON i.object_id = t.object_id WHERE i.is_disabled = 1 AND t.is_ms_shipped = 0

displayName: Detect untrusted foreign key and CHECK constraints â€” optimizer ignores them, potentially missing better plans
description: Constraints that are not trusted (is_not_trusted = 1) are ignored by the query optimizer. This happens when constraints are enabled without checking existing data (WITH NOCHECK). The optimizer cannot use these constraints for cardinality estimation, potentially producing suboptimal plans. Re-enable with WITH CHECK CHECK CONSTRAINT to re-establish trust.
message: Review: Detect untrusted foreign key and CHECK constraints â€” optimizer ignores them, potentially missing better plans

displayName: Check statistics sampling rate â€” statistics with less than 25% sampling may produce inaccurate cardinality estimates

displayName: Detect untrusted foreign key and CHECK constraints â€” optimizer ignores them, potentially missing better execution plans
description: Constraints that are not trusted (is_not_trusted = 1) are ignored by the query optimizer. This happens when constraints are enabled without checking existing data (WITH NOCHECK). The optimizer cannot use these constraints for cardinality estimation, potentially producing suboptimal plans. Re-enable with WITH CHECK CHECK CONSTRAINT to re-establish trust.
message: Review: Detect untrusted foreign key and CHECK constraints â€” optimizer ignores them, potentially missing better execution plans
sql_query: SELECT QUOTENAME(OBJECT_SCHEMA_NAME(FKC.parent_object_id)) + '.' + QUOTENAME(OBJECT_NAME(FKC.parent_object_id)) + '.' + QUOTENAME(FKC.name) AS ConstraintName, 'FOREIGN KEY' AS ConstraintType FROM sys.foreign_keys FKC INNER JOIN sys.objects o ON FKC.parent_object_id = o.[object_id] WHERE o.type = 'U' AND FKC.is_not_trusted = 1 AND FKC.is_not_for_replication = 0 AND o.[is_ms_shipped] = 0 UNION ALL SELECT QUOTENAME(OBJECT_SCHEMA_NAME(CC.parent_object_id)) + '.' + QUOTENAME(OBJECT_NAME(CC.parent_object_id)) + '.' + QUOTENAME(CC.name), 'CHECK' FROM sys.check_constraints CC INNER JOIN sys.objects o ON CC.parent_object_id = o.[object_id] WHERE o.type = 'U' AND CC.is_not_trusted = 1 AND CC.is_not_for_replication = 0 AND CC.is_disabled = 0 AND o.[is_ms_shipped] = 0
expected_result: All constraints trusted â€” no is_not_trusted = 1
condition: SELECT QUOTENAME(OBJECT_SCHEMA_NAME(FKC.parent_object_id)) + '.' + QUOTENAME(OBJECT_NAME(FKC.parent_object_id)) + '.' + QUOTENAME(FKC.name) AS ConstraintName, 'FOREIGN KEY' AS ConstraintType FROM sys.foreign_keys FKC INNER JOIN sys.objects o ON FKC.parent_object_id = o.[object_id] WHERE o.type = 'U' AND FKC.is_not_trusted = 1 AND FKC.is_not_for_replication = 0 AND o.[is_ms_shipped] = 0 UNION ALL SELECT QUOTENAME(OBJECT_SCHEMA_NAME(CC.parent_object_id)) + '.' + QUOTENAME(OBJECT_NAME(CC.parent_object_id)) + '.' + QUOTENAME(CC.name), 'CHECK' FROM sys.check_constraints CC INNER JOIN sys.objects o ON CC.parent_object_id = o.[object_id] WHERE o.type = 'U' AND CC.is_not_trusted = 1 AND CC.is_not_for_replication = 0 AND CC.is_disabled = 0 AND o.[is_ms_shipped] = 0
enhancement_notes: Converted from basic format - condition logic needs migration to structured expressions

displayName: Detect GUID (uniqueidentifier) in clustered index key â€” causes severe fragmentation due to random insert order
description: GUIDs are random values that cause new rows to be inserted at random positions in the clustered index, leading to page splits and severe fragmentation. Sequential GUIDs (NEWSEQUENTIALID()) partially mitigate this but are still suboptimal. Use identity columns or other sequential keys for clustered indexes.
message: Review: Detect GUID (uniqueidentifier) in clustered index key â€” causes severe fragmentation due to random insert order
sql_query: SELECT QUOTENAME(ss.[name]) + '.' + QUOTENAME(t.[name]) + '.' + QUOTENAME(i.[name]) AS IndexName FROM sys.indexes AS i INNER JOIN sys.tables AS t ON t.[object_id] = i.[object_id] INNER JOIN sys.schemas AS ss ON ss.[schema_id] = t.[schema_id] INNER JOIN sys.index_columns AS sic ON sic.[object_id] = t.[object_id] AND sic.index_id = i.index_id INNER JOIN sys.columns AS sc ON sc.[object_id] = t.[object_id] AND sc.column_id = sic.column_id INNER JOIN sys.types AS sty ON sc.user_type_id = sty.user_type_id WHERE sic.is_included_column = 0 AND sty.name = 'uniqueidentifier' AND i.type = 1 AND i.is_unique_constraint = 0 AND t.is_ms_shipped = 0
expected_result: No GUID columns in clustered index keys
condition: SELECT QUOTENAME(ss.[name]) + '.' + QUOTENAME(t.[name]) + '.' + QUOTENAME(i.[name]) AS IndexName FROM sys.indexes AS i INNER JOIN sys.tables AS t ON t.[object_id] = i.[object_id] INNER JOIN sys.schemas AS ss ON ss.[schema_id] = t.[schema_id] INNER JOIN sys.index_columns AS sic ON sic.[object_id] = t.[object_id] AND sic.index_id = i.index_id INNER JOIN sys.columns AS sc ON sc.[object_id] = t.[object_id] AND sc.column_id = sic.column_id INNER JOIN sys.types AS sty ON sc.user_type_id = sty.user_type_id WHERE sic.is_included_column = 0 AND sty.name = 'uniqueidentifier' AND i.type = 1 AND i.is_unique_constraint = 0 AND t.is_ms_shipped = 0
enhancement_notes: Converted from basic format - condition logic needs migration to structured expressions

displayName: Detect duplicate indexes (same key columns) â€” waste storage, memory, and write performance with no query benefit
description: Duplicate indexes have identical key column sets. Each duplicate must be maintained during INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE operations, doubling write overhead for no query benefit. The optimizer may also choose the wrong index, causing plan variability.
message: Review: Detect duplicate indexes (same key columns) â€” waste storage, memory, and write performance with no query benefit
sql_query: SELECT QUOTENAME(OBJECT_SCHEMA_NAME(i1.object_id)) + '.' + QUOTENAME(OBJECT_NAME(i1.object_id)) AS TableName, QUOTENAME(i1.name) AS Index1, QUOTENAME(i2.name) AS Index2 FROM sys.indexes i1 INNER JOIN sys.indexes i2 ON i1.object_id = i2.object_id AND i1.index_id < i2.index_id WHERE i1.type IN (1, 2) AND i2.type IN (1, 2) AND i1.is_primary_key = 0 AND i2.is_primary_key = 0 AND i1.is_unique_constraint = 0 AND i2.is_unique_constraint = 0 AND NOT EXISTS ( SELECT ic1.column_id, ic1.is_included_column EXCEPT SELECT ic2.column_id, ic2.is_included_column FROM sys.index_columns ic1 INNER JOIN sys.index_columns ic2 ON ic1.object_id = ic2.object_id AND ic1.index_id = i1.index_id AND ic2.index_id = i2.index_id AND ic1.column_id = ic2.column_id AND ic1.is_included_column = ic2.is_included_column WHERE ic1.object_id = i1.object_id )
expected_result: No duplicate indexes on user tables
condition: SELECT QUOTENAME(OBJECT_SCHEMA_NAME(i1.object_id)) + '.' + QUOTENAME(OBJECT_NAME(i1.object_id)) AS TableName, QUOTENAME(i1.name) AS Index1, QUOTENAME(i2.name) AS Index2 FROM sys.indexes i1 INNER JOIN sys.indexes i2 ON i1.object_id = i2.object_id AND i1.index_id < i2.index_id WHERE i1.type IN (1, 2) AND i2.type IN (1, 2) AND i1.is_primary_key = 0 AND i2.is_primary_key = 0 AND i1.is_unique_constraint = 0 AND i2.is_unique_constraint = 0 AND NOT EXISTS ( SELECT ic1.column_id, ic1.is_included_column EXCEPT SELECT ic2.column_id, ic2.is_included_column FROM sys.index_columns ic1 INNER JOIN sys.index_columns ic2 ON ic1.object_id = ic2.object_id AND ic1.index_id = i1.index_id AND ic2.index_id = i2.index_id AND ic1.column_id = ic2.column_id AND ic1.is_included_column = ic2.is_included_column WHERE ic1.object_id = i1.object_id )
enhancement_notes: Converted from basic format - condition logic needs migration to structured expressions

displayName: Detect poison waits â€” specific wait types associated with severe systemic performance problems (THREADPOOL, RESOURCE_SEMAPHORE, LOG_RATE_GOVERNOR, etc.)
description: Poison waits are a subset of wait types that indicate systemic problems rather than normal workload waits. These should trigger immediate investigation when exceeding baseline thresholds.
message: Review: Detect poison waits â€” specific wait types associated with severe systemic performance problems (THREADPOOL, RESOURCE_SEMAPHORE, LOG_RATE_GOVERNOR, etc.)
sql_query: SELECT wait_type, wait_time_ms / 1000.0 AS wait_time_seconds, waiting_tasks_count, signal_wait_time_ms / 1000.0 AS signal_wait_seconds FROM sys.dm_os_wait_stats WHERE wait_type IN ('THREADPOOL','RESOURCE_SEMAPHORE','RESOURCE_SEMAPHORE_QUERY_COMPILE','LOG_RATE_GOVERNOR','POOL_LOG_RATE_GOVERNOR','IO_QUEUE_LIMIT','IO_RETRY','RESMGR_THROTTLED') AND wait_time_ms > 60000 ORDER BY wait_time_ms DESC
expected_result: No poison waits exceeding baseline thresholds
condition: SELECT wait_type, wait_time_ms / 1000.0 AS wait_time_seconds, waiting_tasks_count, signal_wait_time_ms / 1000.0 AS signal_wait_seconds FROM sys.dm_os_wait_stats WHERE wait_type IN ('THREADPOOL','RESOURCE_SEMAPHORE','RESOURCE_SEMAPHORE_QUERY_COMPILE','LOG_RATE_GOVERNOR','POOL_LOG_RATE_GOVERNOR','IO_QUEUE_LIMIT','IO_RETRY','RESMGR_THROTTLED') AND wait_time_ms > 60000 ORDER BY wait_time_ms DESC

displayName: Detect TempDB with more than 16 data files â€” exceeding 16 rarely provides additional benefit and may indicate unanalyzed configuration
description: While multiple TempDB data files reduce allocation page contention, exceeding 16 files rarely provides additional benefit and may indicate the configuration was not analyzed against the actual number of cores. The rule of thumb is 1 file per CPU up to 8, then add in groups of 4.
message: Review: Detect TempDB with more than 16 data files â€” exceeding 16 rarely provides additional benefit and may indicate unanalyzed configuration
sql_query: SELECT COUNT(*) AS file_count FROM sys.master_files WHERE database_id = 2 AND type = 0
expected_result: TempDB data file count is appropriate for CPU count (not exceeding 16 unless justified)
condition: SELECT COUNT(*) AS file_count FROM sys.master_files WHERE database_id = 2 AND type = 0
enhancement_notes: Converted from basic format - condition logic needs migration to structured expressions

displayName: Detect excessive Extended Events sessions â€” too many active sessions add overhead as each evaluates and captures events matching its filters
description: An unusually high number of non-system Extended Events sessions (excluding system_health, AlwaysOn_health, telemetry_xevents, sp_server_diagnostics, hkenginexesession) can add measurable overhead. Two or more custom sessions warrant review.
message: Review: Detect excessive Extended Events sessions â€” too many active sessions add overhead as each evaluates and captures events matching its filters
sql_query: SELECT name, create_time FROM sys.dm_xe_sessions WHERE name NOT IN ('AlwaysOn_health','system_health','telemetry_xevents','sp_server_diagnostics','sp_server_diagnostics session','hkenginexesession') AND name NOT LIKE '%$A%'
expected_result: Minimal custom Extended Events sessions â€” all documented and necessary
condition: SELECT name, create_time FROM sys.dm_xe_sessions WHERE name NOT IN ('AlwaysOn_health','system_health','telemetry_xevents','sp_server_diagnostics','sp_server_diagnostics session','hkenginexesession') AND name NOT LIKE '%$A%'
enhancement_notes: Converted from basic format - condition logic needs migration to structured expressions

displayName: Detect disabled internal monitoring features â€” -x startup parameter disables default trace and system health session, removing critical diagnostic data
description: The -x startup parameter disables the default trace and system health Extended Events session. These features provide valuable diagnostic data for troubleshooting configuration changes, deadlocks, errors, and performance issues. Disabling them removes critical visibility.
message: Review: Detect disabled internal monitoring features â€” -x startup parameter disables default trace and system health session, removing critical diagnostic data
sql_query: SELECT value_data FROM sys.dm_server_registry WHERE registry_key LIKE '%MSSQLServer\Parameters' AND value_data = '-x'
expected_result: No -x startup parameter â€” default trace and system_health enabled
condition: SELECT value_data FROM sys.dm_server_registry WHERE registry_key LIKE '%MSSQLServer\Parameters' AND value_data = '-x'
enhancement_notes: Converted from basic format - condition logic needs migration to structured expressions

displayName: Detect volume snapshot backups â€” large backups completing in under 60 seconds indicate VSS or third-party snapshot technology in use
description: Full backups of 50GB+ completing in under 60 seconds indicate volume snapshot technology (VSS, SAN snapshots, etc.). While valid, snapshot backups require proper SQL Server integration to ensure log truncation occurs and I/O is properly quiesced.
message: Review: Detect volume snapshot backups â€” large backups completing in under 60 seconds indicate VSS or third-party snapshot technology in use
sql_query: SELECT COUNT(*) AS snapshot_backup_count FROM msdb.dbo.backupset WHERE type = 'D' AND backup_size >= 50000000000 AND DATEDIFF(SECOND, backup_start_date, backup_finish_date) <= 60 AND backup_finish_date >= DATEADD(DAY, -14, GETDATE())
expected_result: Snapshot backups documented and properly integrated with SQL Server
condition: SELECT COUNT(*) AS snapshot_backup_count FROM msdb.dbo.backupset WHERE type = 'D' AND backup_size >= 50000000000 AND DATEDIFF(SECOND, backup_start_date, backup_finish_date) <= 60 AND backup_finish_date >= DATEADD(DAY, -14, GETDATE())
enhancement_notes: Converted from basic format - condition logic needs migration to structured expressions

displayName: Detect dangerous third-party modules loaded into SQL Server process â€” known to cause stability and performance issues (McAfee, Sophos, CrowdStrike, etc.)
description: Third-party modules (DLLs) loaded into the SQL Server process can cause crashes, hangs, and performance degradation. Known problematic modules include McAfee (ENTAPI.DLL, MFEBOPK.SYS, MFETDIK.SYS), Sophos (SOPHOS_DETOURED.DLL, SWI_IFSLSP_64.dll), CrowdStrike (ScriptControl*.dll, umppc*.dll), Trend Micro (perfiCrcPerfMonMgr.DLL), and others listed in Microsoft KB 2033238.
message: Review: Detect dangerous third-party modules loaded into SQL Server process â€” known to cause stability and performance issues (McAfee, Sophos, CrowdStrike, etc.)
sql_query: SELECT company, description, name FROM sys.dm_os_loaded_modules WHERE UPPER(name) LIKE '%ENTAPI.DLL' OR UPPER(name) LIKE '%MFEBOPK.SYS' OR UPPER(name) LIKE '%HIPI.DLL' OR UPPER(name) LIKE '%HcSQL.dll' OR UPPER(name) LIKE '%SOPHOS_DETOURED.DLL' OR UPPER(name) LIKE '%SWI_IFSLSP_64.dll' OR UPPER(name) LIKE '%PIOLEDB.DLL' OR UPPER(name) LIKE '%ScriptControl%.dll' OR UPPER(name) LIKE '%umppc%.dll' OR UPPER(name) LIKE '%perfiCrcPerfMonMgr.DLL' OR UPPER(name) LIKE '%NLEMSQL.SYS' OR UPPER(name) LIKE '%MFETDIK.SYS' OR UPPER(name) LIKE '%ANTIVIRUS%'
expected_result: No dangerous third-party modules loaded into SQL Server process
condition: SELECT company, description, name FROM sys.dm_os_loaded_modules WHERE UPPER(name) LIKE '%ENTAPI.DLL' OR UPPER(name) LIKE '%MFEBOPK.SYS' OR UPPER(name) LIKE '%HIPI.DLL' OR UPPER(name) LIKE '%HcSQL.dll' OR UPPER(name) LIKE '%SOPHOS_DETOURED.DLL' OR UPPER(name) LIKE '%SWI_IFSLSP_64.dll' OR UPPER(name) LIKE '%PIOLEDB.DLL' OR UPPER(name) LIKE '%ScriptControl%.dll' OR UPPER(name) LIKE '%umppc%.dll' OR UPPER(name) LIKE '%perfiCrcPerfMonMgr.DLL' OR UPPER(name) LIKE '%NLEMSQL.SYS' OR UPPER(name) LIKE '%MFETDIK.SYS' OR UPPER(name) LIKE '%ANTIVIRUS%'
enhancement_notes: Converted from basic format - condition logic needs migration to structured expressions

displayName: Detect Shrink Database step in SQL Server Maintenance Plans â€” causes severe fragmentation requiring immediate rebuilds
description: Maintenance Plans containing a 'Shrink Database Task' cause severe index fragmentation, waste I/O as freed space is quickly re-consumed, and indicate outdated maintenance practices. Modern solutions like Ola Hallengren's scripts should be used instead.
message: Review: Detect Shrink Database step in SQL Server Maintenance Plans â€” causes severe fragmentation requiring immediate rebuilds
sql_query: SELECT name FROM msdb.dbo.sysssispackages WHERE packagetype = 6 AND CAST(CAST(packagedata AS VARBINARY(MAX)) AS XML).exist('//dts:Executable[@dts:ObjectName="Shrink Database Task"]') = 1
expected_result: No maintenance plans with Shrink Database steps
condition: SELECT name FROM msdb.dbo.sysssispackages WHERE packagetype = 6 AND CAST(CAST(packagedata AS VARBINARY(MAX)) AS XML).exist('//dts:Executable[@dts:ObjectName="Shrink Database Task"]') = 1
enhancement_notes: Converted from basic format - condition logic needs migration to structured expressions

displayName: Detect repetitive steps in SQL Server Maintenance Plans â€” rebuilding AND reorganizing indexes on the same database wastes maintenance window time
description: Maintenance Plans containing both Rebuild and Reorganize steps, or Rebuild and Update Statistics steps, perform redundant work. Rebuilding indexes already updates statistics. These repetitive operations waste maintenance window time and system resources.
message: Review: Detect repetitive steps in SQL Server Maintenance Plans â€” rebuilding AND reorganizing indexes on the same database wastes maintenance window time
sql_query: SELECT name FROM msdb.dbo.sysssispackages WHERE packagetype = 6 AND CAST(CAST(packagedata AS VARBINARY(MAX)) AS XML).exist('//dts:Executable[(@dts:ObjectName="Rebuild Index Task" or @dts:ObjectName="Reorganize Index Task")]') = 1
expected_result: No maintenance plans with redundant rebuild/reorganize steps
condition: SELECT name FROM msdb.dbo.sysssispackages WHERE packagetype = 6 AND CAST(CAST(packagedata AS VARBINARY(MAX)) AS XML).exist('//dts:Executable[(@dts:ObjectName="Rebuild Index Task" or @dts:ObjectName="Reorganize Index Task")]') = 1
enhancement_notes: Converted from basic format - condition logic needs migration to structured expressions

displayName: Detect Query Store cleanup disabled â€” SQL 2016 RTM bug causes dumps on cleanup; fixed in CU1
description: SQL 2016 RTM (before CU1) has a bug where Query Store cleanup jobs cause dumps. On Standard/Developer editions running SQL 2016 without CU1, this can cause instability. Verify the instance is patched to at least CU1.
message: Review: Detect Query Store cleanup disabled â€” SQL 2016 RTM bug causes dumps on cleanup; fixed in CU1
sql_query: SELECT @@VERSION AS version, SERVERPROPERTY('ProductVersion') AS product_version WHERE CAST(SERVERPROPERTY('ProductVersion') AS NVARCHAR(128)) LIKE '13%' AND CAST(SERVERPROPERTY('ProductVersion') AS NVARCHAR(128)) NOT LIKE '13.0.2149%'
expected_result: SQL 2016 patched to CU1 or later (build >= 13.0.2149)
condition: SELECT @@VERSION AS version, SERVERPROPERTY('ProductVersion') AS product_version WHERE CAST(SERVERPROPERTY('ProductVersion') AS NVARCHAR(128)) LIKE '13%' AND CAST(SERVERPROPERTY('ProductVersion') AS NVARCHAR(128)) NOT LIKE '13.0.2149%'
enhancement_notes: Converted from basic format - condition logic needs migration to structured expressions

displayName: Detect failover cluster with no available nodes â€” if the active node fails, automatic failover cannot occur
description: A Windows Server Failover Cluster (WSFC) with no available passive nodes cannot fail over if the current active node fails. Check sys.dm_os_cluster_nodes for nodes with status = 0 and is_current_owner = 0.
message: Review: Detect failover cluster with no available nodes â€” if the active node fails, automatic failover cannot occur
sql_query: SELECT node_name, status, is_current_owner, status_description FROM sys.dm_os_cluster_nodes
expected_result: At least one passive node available for failover
condition: SELECT node_name, status, is_current_owner, status_description FROM sys.dm_os_cluster_nodes
enhancement_notes: Converted from basic format - condition logic needs migration to structured expressions

displayName: Detect MSDB backup history purged too frequently â€” removes ability to verify backup chains and identify gaps over time
description: Overly aggressive backup history purging (daily or more) removes the ability to verify backup chains, audit backup history, or identify gaps in backup coverage over time. Retain at least 30-90 days of backup history.
message: Review: Detect MSDB backup history purged too frequently â€” removes ability to verify backup chains and identify gaps over time
sql_query: SELECT MIN(backup_start_date) AS oldest_backup_history, DATEDIFF(DAY, MIN(backup_start_date), GETDATE()) AS days_retained FROM msdb.dbo.backupset
expected_result: Backup history retained for at least 30 days
condition: SELECT MIN(backup_start_date) AS oldest_backup_history, DATEDIFF(DAY, MIN(backup_start_date), GETDATE()) AS days_retained FROM msdb.dbo.backupset
enhancement_notes: Converted from basic format - condition logic needs migration to structured expressions

displayName: Detect database mirroring/AG endpoints owned by users instead of service accounts â€” if owner login is disabled, HA stops working
description: Endpoints owned by user accounts (not the service account) can cause HA failures if the owner login is disabled, deleted, or unavailable due to Active Directory problems. Endpoints should be owned by the SQL Server service account.
message: Review: Detect database mirroring/AG endpoints owned by users instead of service accounts â€” if owner login is disabled, HA stops working
sql_query: SELECT ep.name AS endpoint_name, SUSER_NAME(ep.principal_id) AS owner FROM sys.database_mirroring_endpoints ep LEFT OUTER JOIN sys.dm_server_services s ON SUSER_NAME(ep.principal_id) = s.service_account WHERE s.service_account IS NULL AND ep.principal_id <> 1
expected_result: All HA endpoints owned by SQL Server service account
condition: SELECT ep.name AS endpoint_name, SUSER_NAME(ep.principal_id) AS owner FROM sys.database_mirroring_endpoints ep LEFT OUTER JOIN sys.dm_server_services s ON SUSER_NAME(ep.principal_id) = s.service_account WHERE s.service_account IS NULL AND ep.principal_id <> 1
enhancement_notes: Converted from basic format - condition logic needs migration to structured expressions

displayName: Detect min server memory equal to max server memory â€” prevents dynamic memory management and can starve other processes
description: When min server memory equals max server memory, SQL Server cannot release memory back to the OS when not needed. This prevents dynamic memory management and can starve other processes on the server, especially on shared hosts.
message: Review: Detect min server memory equal to max server memory â€” prevents dynamic memory management and can starve other processes
sql_query: SELECT CAST(value_in_use AS BIGINT) AS min_memory_mb FROM sys.configurations WHERE name = 'min server memory (MB)' INTERSECT SELECT CAST(value_in_use AS BIGINT) FROM sys.configurations WHERE name = 'max server memory (MB)'
expected_result: Min server memory is less than max server memory (allowing dynamic memory)
condition: SELECT CAST(value_in_use AS BIGINT) AS min_memory_mb FROM sys.configurations WHERE name = 'min server memory (MB)' INTERSECT SELECT CAST(value_in_use AS BIGINT) FROM sys.configurations WHERE name = 'max server memory (MB)'
enhancement_notes: Converted from basic format - condition logic needs migration to structured expressions

displayName: Detect TempDB file error â€” mismatch between sys.master_files and tempdb.sys.database_files indicates inaccessible or missing TempDB files
description: A mismatch between the number of TempDB files in sys.master_files (database_id = 2) and tempdb.sys.database_files indicates that one or more TempDB files cannot be found, is inaccessible, or has experienced an I/O error. TempDB errors can cause widespread query failures.
message: Review: Detect TempDB file error â€” mismatch between sys.master_files and tempdb.sys.database_files indicates inaccessible or missing TempDB files
sql_query: SELECT (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM sys.master_files WHERE database_id = 2) AS master_file_count, (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM tempdb.sys.database_files) AS tempdb_file_count
expected_result: Master file count matches tempdb.sys.database_files count
condition: SELECT (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM sys.master_files WHERE database_id = 2) AS master_file_count, (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM tempdb.sys.database_files) AS tempdb_file_count
enhancement_notes: Converted from basic format - condition logic needs migration to structured expressions

displayName: Detect significant DBCC events from default trace â€” frequent DBCC SHRINKFILE, DROPCLEANBUFFERS, FREEPROCCACHE indicate improper practices
description: The default trace captures DBCC command executions. Excluding benign commands (CHECKDB, CHECKTABLE, LOGINFO, etc.), significant DBCC events indicate potentially harmful operations like SHRINKFILE, DROPCLEANBUFFERS, or FREEPROCCACHE being run in production.
message: Review: Detect significant DBCC events from default trace â€” frequent DBCC SHRINKFILE, DROPCLEANBUFFERS, FREEPROCCACHE indicate improper practices
sql_query: SELECT TextData, StartTime, LoginName, ApplicationName FROM sys.fn_trace_gettable((SELECT path FROM sys.traces WHERE is_default = 1), DEFAULT) WHERE EventClass = 116 AND StartTime >= DATEADD(DAY, -7, GETDATE())
expected_result: No harmful DBCC events in default trace (excluding benign commands)
condition: SELECT TextData, StartTime, LoginName, ApplicationName FROM sys.fn_trace_gettable((SELECT path FROM sys.traces WHERE is_default = 1), DEFAULT) WHERE EventClass = 116 AND StartTime >= DATEADD(DAY, -7, GETDATE())
enhancement_notes: Converted from basic format - condition logic needs migration to structured expressions

displayName: Detect DBCC FREEPROCCACHE executed recently â€” forces all queries to recompile, causing temporary high CPU from mass recompilation
description: DBCC FREEPROCCACHE clears all cached execution plans, forcing every query to recompile on next execution. When detected in the default trace, it identifies who ran the command and when. This is distinct from the plan cache being cleared by restart (BLITZ_125).
message: Review: Detect DBCC FREEPROCCACHE executed recently â€” forces all queries to recompile, causing temporary high CPU from mass recompilation
sql_query: SELECT TextData, StartTime, LoginName, NTUserName FROM sys.fn_trace_gettable((SELECT path FROM sys.traces WHERE is_default = 1), DEFAULT) WHERE EventClass = 116 AND UPPER(TextData) = 'DBCC FREEPROCCACHE' AND StartTime >= DATEADD(DAY, -7, GETDATE())
expected_result: No DBCC FREEPROCCACHE executions in default trace
condition: SELECT TextData, StartTime, LoginName, NTUserName FROM sys.fn_trace_gettable((SELECT path FROM sys.traces WHERE is_default = 1), DEFAULT) WHERE EventClass = 116 AND UPPER(TextData) = 'DBCC FREEPROCCACHE' AND StartTime >= DATEADD(DAY, -7, GETDATE())
enhancement_notes: Converted from basic format - condition logic needs migration to structured expressions

displayName: Detect databases with unknown owner â€” SUSER_SNAME(owner_sid) returns NULL, indicating deleted or renamed owner account
description: When the database owner SID does not correspond to any valid SQL Server login, SUSER_SNAME(owner_sid) returns NULL. This indicates the original owner account has been deleted or renamed, causing permission issues and potential security gaps.
message: Review: Detect databases with unknown owner â€” SUSER_SNAME(owner_sid) returns NULL, indicating deleted or renamed owner account
sql_query: SELECT name, SUSER_SNAME(owner_sid) AS owner FROM sys.databases WHERE SUSER_SNAME(owner_sid) IS NULL
expected_result: No databases with unknown/NULL owner
condition: SELECT name, SUSER_SNAME(owner_sid) AS owner FROM sys.databases WHERE SUSER_SNAME(owner_sid) IS NULL
enhancement_notes: Converted from basic format - condition logic needs migration to structured expressions

displayName: Detect implicit transactions â€” connections using IMPLICIT_TRANSACTIONS mode cause long-running open transactions and blocking
description: With implicit transactions, every DML statement automatically starts an open transaction that must be explicitly committed or rolled back. If not properly managed, this causes long-running transactions, excessive blocking, and log growth. Check sys.dm_tran_active_transactions for name = 'implicit_transaction'.
message: Review: Detect implicit transactions â€” connections using IMPLICIT_TRANSACTIONS mode cause long-running open transactions and blocking
sql_query: SELECT DB_NAME(s.database_id) AS database_name, COUNT_BIG(*) AS implicit_tran_count, MIN(tat.transaction_begin_time) AS oldest_begin FROM sys.dm_tran_active_transactions tat LEFT JOIN sys.dm_tran_session_transactions tst ON tst.transaction_id = tat.transaction_id LEFT JOIN sys.dm_exec_sessions s ON s.session_id = tst.session_id WHERE tat.name = 'implicit_transaction' GROUP BY DB_NAME(s.database_id)
expected_result: No active implicit transactions (or documented and managed)
condition: SELECT DB_NAME(s.database_id) AS database_name, COUNT_BIG(*) AS implicit_tran_count, MIN(tat.transaction_begin_time) AS oldest_begin FROM sys.dm_tran_active_transactions tat LEFT JOIN sys.dm_tran_session_transactions tst ON tst.transaction_id = tat.transaction_id LEFT JOIN sys.dm_exec_sessions s ON s.session_id = tst.session_id WHERE tat.name = 'implicit_transaction' GROUP BY DB_NAME(s.database_id)
enhancement_notes: Converted from basic format - condition logic needs migration to structured expressions

displayName: Detect objects created with dangerous SET options (ANSI_NULLS OFF or QUOTED_IDENTIFIER OFF) â€” breaks filtered indexes, indexed views, and advanced features
description: Objects created with uses_ansi_nulls = 0 or uses_quoted_identifier = 0 can cause incorrect query results and prevent the use of indexed views, filtered indexes, and computed columns. These settings are deprecated and will be removed in a future SQL Server version.
message: Review: Detect objects created with dangerous SET options (ANSI_NULLS OFF or QUOTED_IDENTIFIER OFF) â€” breaks filtered indexes, indexed views, and advanced features
sql_query: SELECT s.name + '.' + o.name AS object_name, CASE WHEN sm.uses_ansi_nulls = 0 THEN 'ANSI_NULLS OFF' ELSE '' END + CASE WHEN sm.uses_quoted_identifier = 0 THEN ' QUOTED_IDENTIFIER OFF' ELSE '' END AS bad_options FROM sys.sql_modules sm JOIN sys.objects o ON o.object_id = sm.object_id JOIN sys.schemas s ON o.schema_id = s.schema_id WHERE (sm.uses_ansi_nulls <> 1 OR sm.uses_quoted_identifier <> 1) AND o.is_ms_shipped = 0
expected_result: No user objects with ANSI_NULLS OFF or QUOTED_IDENTIFIER OFF
condition: SELECT s.name + '.' + o.name AS object_name, CASE WHEN sm.uses_ansi_nulls = 0 THEN 'ANSI_NULLS OFF' ELSE '' END + CASE WHEN sm.uses_quoted_identifier = 0 THEN ' QUOTED_IDENTIFIER OFF' ELSE '' END AS bad_options FROM sys.sql_modules sm JOIN sys.objects o ON o.object_id = sm.object_id JOIN sys.schemas s ON o.schema_id = s.schema_id WHERE (sm.uses_ansi_nulls <> 1 OR sm.uses_quoted_identifier <> 1) AND o.is_ms_shipped = 0
enhancement_notes: Converted from basic format - condition logic needs migration to structured expressions

displayName: Detect SQL Server Agent alerts without event descriptions in output â€” alerts missing include_event_description = 1 provide less useful notifications
description: Alerts configured with include_event_description = 0 do not include the event description in their notification messages. This makes it harder for DBAs to understand and respond to the alert. Enable 'Include alert error text in the e-mail' in alert properties.
message: Review: Detect SQL Server Agent alerts without event descriptions in output â€” alerts missing include_event_description = 1 provide less useful notifications
sql_query: SELECT name, severity, include_event_description FROM msdb.dbo.sysalerts WHERE enabled = 1 AND include_event_description = 0
expected_result: All enabled alerts have include_event_description = 1
condition: SELECT name, severity, include_event_description FROM msdb.dbo.sysalerts WHERE enabled = 1 AND include_event_description = 0

displayName: Databases Not Backed Up Recently
description: Databases that have not had a full backup in the last 7 days
sql_query:
SELECT d.name AS DatabaseName, 
       COALESCE(CAST(MAX(b.backup_finish_date) AS VARCHAR(25)), 'never') AS LastBackup
FROM master.sys.databases d
LEFT OUTER JOIN msdb.dbo.backupset b ON d.name = b.database_name 
    AND b.type = 'D' 
    AND b.server_name = SERVERPROPERTY('ServerName')
WHERE d.database_id <> 2 
    AND d.state NOT IN (1, 6, 10) 
    AND d.is_in_standby = 0 
    AND d.source_database_id IS NULL
GROUP BY d.name
HAVING MAX(b.backup_finish_date) <= DATEADD(dd, -7, GETDATE()) 
    OR MAX(b.backup_finish_date) IS NULL
expected_result: No databases should appear - all should have recent backups
enhancement_notes: Added from sp_Blitz analysis - SQL query extracted and adapted for SQL 2016+
framework_alignment: Critical for business continuity and data protection
community_validation: Aligned with CIS, NIST, ISO27001, and STIG frameworks for enterprise reliability

displayName: Databases in SIMPLE Recovery Without Log Backups

FROM master.sys.databases d
WHERE d.recovery_model_desc = 'SIMPLE'
    AND d.database_id > 4
    AND EXISTS (
        SELECT 1 FROM msdb.dbo.backupset b 
        WHERE b.database_name = d.name 
        AND b.type = 'L'

framework_alignment: Recovery model consistency check

displayName: Databases in FULL Recovery Without Recent Log Backups
description: Databases in FULL recovery that have not had log backups in last week
sql_query:
SELECT d.name AS DatabaseName, 
       COALESCE(CAST(MAX(b.backup_finish_date) AS VARCHAR(25)), 'never') AS LastLogBackup
FROM master.sys.databases d

WHERE d.recovery_model_desc = 'FULL'

displayName: TF 174 increases plan cache bucket count
enhancement_notes: Extracted from official Microsoft DefaultRuleset.csv - comprehensive enterprise ruleset
sql_query: -- MANUAL SQL DEVELOPMENT NEEDED:  Microsoft Assessment API rule - SQL extraction requires API evaluation
message: Review: TF 174 increases plan cache bucket count
expected_result: Rule evaluation via Microsoft SQL Assessment API
description: Trace Flag 174 increases the SQL Server plan cache bucket count from 40,009 to 160,001 on 64-bit systems. When the SQL Server plan cache reaches its entry limit, plans that have low cost must be evicted in order to insert new plans. This can cause severe contention on the SOS_CACHESTORE spinlock and a high CPU usage occurs in SQL Server. On 64-bit systems, the number of buckets for the SQL Server plan cache is 40,009. Therefore, the maximum number of entries that can fit inside the SQL Server plan cache is 160,036. Enabling trace flag 174 on high performance systems increases the size of the cache and can avoid SOS_CACHESTORE spinlock contention.
community_validation: Official Microsoft SQL Assessment API rules - enterprise-grade reliability

displayName: TF 634 disables background columnstore compression
enhancement_notes: Extracted from official Microsoft DefaultRuleset.csv - comprehensive enterprise ruleset
sql_query: -- MANUAL SQL DEVELOPMENT NEEDED:  Microsoft Assessment API rule - SQL extraction requires API evaluation
message: Review: TF 634 disables background columnstore compression
expected_result: Rule evaluation via Microsoft SQL Assessment API
description: Trace Flag 634 disables the background columnstore compression task. SQL Server periodically runs the Tuple Mover background task that compresses columnstore index rowgroups with uncompressed data, one such rowgroup at a time. Columnstore compression improves query performance but also consumes system resources. You can control the timing of columnstore compression manually, by disabling the background compression task with trace flag 634, and then explicitly invoking ALTER INDEX...REORGANIZE or ALTER INDEX...REBUILD at the time of your choice.
community_validation: Official Microsoft SQL Assessment API rules - enterprise-grade reliability

displayName: TF 652 disables page pre-fetching scans
enhancement_notes: Extracted from official Microsoft DefaultRuleset.csv - comprehensive enterprise ruleset
sql_query: -- MANUAL SQL DEVELOPMENT NEEDED:  Microsoft Assessment API rule - SQL extraction requires API evaluation
message: Review: TF 652 disables page pre-fetching scans
expected_result: Rule evaluation via Microsoft SQL Assessment API
description: Trace Flag 652 disables page pre-fetching for scans. The read-ahead process reads the full extent of a referenced database page into the buffer pool, before those contiguous database pages are consumed by scans. Enabling the trace flag can lead to performance issues on queries that leverage pre-fetching.
community_validation: Official Microsoft SQL Assessment API rules - enterprise-grade reliability

displayName: TF 661 disables background ghost cleanup task
enhancement_notes: Extracted from official Microsoft DefaultRuleset.csv - comprehensive enterprise ruleset
sql_query: -- MANUAL SQL DEVELOPMENT NEEDED:  Microsoft Assessment API rule - SQL extraction requires API evaluation
message: Review: TF 661 disables background ghost cleanup task
expected_result: Rule evaluation via Microsoft SQL Assessment API
description: Trace Flag 661 disables the ghost record removal process. A ghost record is the result of a delete operation. When you delete a record, the deleted record is kept as a ghost record. Later, the deleted record is purged by the ghost record removal process. When you disable this process, the deleted record is not purged. Therefore, the space that the deleted record consumes is not freed. This behavior affects space consumption and the performance of scan operations. If you turn off this trace Flag, the ghost record removal process works correctly.
community_validation: Official Microsoft SQL Assessment API rules - enterprise-grade reliability

displayName: TF 834 enables large-page allocations

expected_result: Rule evaluation via Microsoft SQL Assessment API

Trace flag 834 is NOT recommended on SQL Server instances that use columnstore indexes.

description: Trace Flag 1117 initiates the growth of every file in the filegroup, when a file in the filegroup meets the autogrow threshold, and together with trace flag 1118, can help reduce allocation contention in the SQL Server TempDB database. This trace flag affects all databases and is recommended only if every database is safe to be grow all files in a filegroup by the same amount.

displayName: TF 1118 disables single page allocations
enhancement_notes: Extracted from official Microsoft DefaultRuleset.csv - comprehensive enterprise ruleset
sql_query: -- MANUAL SQL DEVELOPMENT NEEDED:  Microsoft Assessment API rule - SQL extraction requires API evaluation
message: Review: TF 1118 disables single page allocations
expected_result: Rule evaluation via Microsoft SQL Assessment API
description: Trace Flag 1118 forces page allocations on uniform extents instead of mixed extents, and together with trace flag 1117, can help reduce allocation contention in the SQL Server TempDB database. When a new object is created, by default, the first eight pages are allocated from different extents (mixed extents). Afterwards, when more pages are needed, those are allocated from that same extent (uniform extent). The SGAM page is used to track these mixed extents, so can quickly become a bottleneck when numerous mixed page allocations are occurring. This trace flag allocates all eight pages from the same extent when creating new objects, minimizing the need to scan the SGAM page and forces uniform extent allocations instead of mixed page allocations.
community_validation: Official Microsoft SQL Assessment API rules - enterprise-grade reliability

description: Trace Flag 1211 disables lock escalation based on memory pressure, or based on number of locks. Because SQL Server will not escalate row or page locks to table locks, this can generate an excessive number of locks. If the lock memory grows large enough, attempts to allocate additional locks for any query may fail and cause performance issues. This trace flag should not be used in order to avoid inability to allocate lock resources because of insufficient memory. If lock escalation needs to be disabled, use trace flag 1224 instead.

message: Review: 'PARAMETERIZATION' should be 'SIMPLE'
expected_result: Rule evaluation via Microsoft SQL Assessment API
description: When the PARAMETERIZATION database option is set to SIMPLE, the SQL Server query optimizer may choose to parameterize the queries. This means that any literal values that are contained in a query are substituted with parameters. This process is referred to as simple parameterization. When SIMPLE parameterization is in effect, you cannot control which queries are parameterized and which queries are not. However, you can specify that all queries in a database be parameterized by setting the PARAMETERIZATION database option to FORCED. Regardless of whether you have a Plan-Stability or not, SQL Server will always auto parameterize your SQL Statements, and reuse the cached execution plan over and over again. It doesn't matter how terrible the execution plan is. Therefore you have to know in exact detail the shape of your execution plans, and whether they can lead to some performance related problems.

displayName: VLF count
enhancement_notes: Extracted from official Microsoft DefaultRuleset.csv - comprehensive enterprise ruleset
sql_query: -- MANUAL SQL DEVELOPMENT NEEDED:  Microsoft Assessment API rule - SQL extraction requires API evaluation
message: Review: VLF count
expected_result: Rule evaluation via Microsoft SQL Assessment API
description: The SQL Server Database Engine divides each physical log file internally into a number of virtual log files (VLFs). Virtual log files have no fixed size, and there is no fixed number of virtual log files for a physical log file. The Database Engine chooses the size of the virtual log files dynamically while it is creating or extending log files. The Database Engine tries to maintain a small number of virtual files. The size of the virtual files after a log file has been extended is the sum of the size of the existing log and the size of the new file increment. The size or number of virtual log files cannot be configured or set by administrators. If the log files grow to a large size in many small increments, they will have many virtual log files. This can slow down database startup and also log backup and restore operations. Conversely, if the log files are set to a large size with few or just one increment, they will have few very large virtual log files. We recommend that you assign log files a size value close to the final size required, using the required increments to achieve optimal VLF distribution, and also have a relatively large growth_increment value.
community_validation: Official Microsoft SQL Assessment API rules - enterprise-grade reliability

description: Microsoft SQL Server is non-uniform memory access (NUMA) aware. NUMA architecture provides a scalable solution to performance problem. Computers with hardware NUMA have more than one system bus, each serving a small set of processors. Each group of processors has its own memory and possibly its own I/O channels, but each CPU can access memory associated with other groups in a coherent way. Each group is called a NUMA node. The number of CPUs within a NUMA node depends on the hardware vendor. Your hardware manufacturer can tell you if your computer supports hardware NUMA.

description: If you need to load a lot of data quickly, you can disable nonclustered indexes in order to improve performance. After the data load finishes, enable the nonclustered indexes again by rebuilding them. This technique works best in large data warehouse environments where entire dimension tables might be reloaded from scratch every night. Disabling nonclustered indexes is safer than dropping and recreating them because scripting indexes is hard. Unfortunately, sometimes our load processes crash before enabling the indexes again, or sometimes we manually load data and we forget to rebuild them.

sql_query: -- MANUAL SQL DEVELOPMENT NEEDED:  Microsoft Assessment API rule - SQL extraction requires API evaluation
message: Review: Index fill factor is below 80%
expected_result: Rule evaluation via Microsoft SQL Assessment API
description: The fill-factor option is provided for fine-tuning index data storage and performance. When an index is created or rebuilt, the fill-factor value determines the percentage of space on each leaf-level page to be filled with data, reserving the remainder on each page as free space for future growth. For example, specifying a fill-factor value of 80 means that 20 percent of each leaf-level page will be left empty, providing space for index expansion as data is added to the underlying table. The empty space is reserved between the index rows rather than at the end of the index.
community_validation: Official Microsoft SQL Assessment API rules - enterprise-grade reliability

displayName: Non-unique clustered indexes
enhancement_notes: Extracted from official Microsoft DefaultRuleset.csv - comprehensive enterprise ruleset
sql_query: -- MANUAL SQL DEVELOPMENT NEEDED:  Microsoft Assessment API rule - SQL extraction requires API evaluation
message: Review: Non-unique clustered indexes
expected_result: Rule evaluation via Microsoft SQL Assessment API
description: Index uniqueness is highly desirable attribute of a clustering key, and goes hand-in-hand with index narrowness. SQL Server does not require a clustered index to be unique, but yet it must have some means of uniquely identifying every row. That’s why, for non-unique clustered indexes, SQL Server adds to every duplicate instance of a clustering key value a 4-byte integer value called a uniqueifier. This uniqueifier is added everywhere the clustering key is stored. That means the uniqueifier is stored in both clustered and non-clustered indexes. As you can imagine, if there are many rows using the same clustering key value, this can become quite expensive.

displayName: Statistics need to be updated
enhancement_notes: Extracted from official Microsoft DefaultRuleset.csv - comprehensive enterprise ruleset
sql_query: -- MANUAL SQL DEVELOPMENT NEEDED:  Microsoft Assessment API rule - SQL extraction requires API evaluation
message: Review: Statistics need to be updated
expected_result: Rule evaluation via Microsoft SQL Assessment API
description: The Query Optimizer determines whether an index is useful for a specific query by evaluating the stored statistics. If the statistics become out of date and significant changes have occurred against the underlying data, this can result in less than optimal query performance. In most cases, it's best to let SQL Server maintain the statistics. If you turn 'Auto Create Stats' and 'Auto Update Stats' off, then it is up to you to keep the statistics up-to-date somehow. Failure to do so will lead to poor query performance. Most applications should have these options ON. When the Auto Update Statistics setting is ON, the Query Optimizer updates statistics when they are used by a query and when they might be out-of-date. Statistics become out-of-date after insert, update, delete, or merge operations change the data distribution in the table or indexed view. The Query Optimizer determines when statistics might be out-of-date by counting the number of data modifications since the last statistics update and comparing the number of modifications to a threshold. The threshold is based on the number of rows in the table or indexed view. The Query Optimizer checks for out-of-date statistics before compiling a query and before executing a cached query plan. Before compiling a query, the Query Optimizer uses the columns, tables, and indexed views in the query predicate to determine which statistics might be out-of-date. Before executing a cached query plan, the Database Engine verifies that the query plan references up-to-date statistics. The AUTO_UPDATE_STATISTICS option applies to statistics created for indexes, single-columns in query predicates, and statistics that are created by using the CREATE STATISTICS statement. This option also applies to filtered statistics.
community_validation: Official Microsoft SQL Assessment API rules - enterprise-grade reliability

enhancement_notes: Extracted from official Microsoft DefaultRuleset.csv - comprehensive enterprise ruleset
sql_query: -- MANUAL SQL DEVELOPMENT NEEDED:  Microsoft Assessment API rule - SQL extraction requires API evaluation
message: Review: Auto Soft NUMA should be enabled
expected_result: Rule evaluation via Microsoft SQL Assessment API
description: Modern processors have multiple cores per socket. Each socket is represented, usually, as a single NUMA node. The SQL Server database engine partitions various internal structures and partitions service threads per NUMA node. With processors containing 10 or more cores per socket, using software NUMA to split hardware NUMA nodes generally increases scalability and performance. Prior to SQL Server 2014 (12.x) SP2, software-based NUMA (soft-NUMA) has required to edit the registry to add a node configuration affinity mask, and was configured at the host level, rather than per instance. Soft-NUMA has configured automatically at the database-instance level when the SQL Server Database Engine service starts.

enhancement_notes: Extracted from official Microsoft DefaultRuleset.csv - comprehensive enterprise ruleset
sql_query: -- MANUAL SQL DEVELOPMENT NEEDED:  Microsoft Assessment API rule - SQL extraction requires API evaluation
message: Review: Option 'cross db ownership chaining' should be disabled
expected_result: Rule evaluation via Microsoft SQL Assessment API
description: Cross-database ownership chaining occurs when a procedure in one database depends on objects in another database. A cross-database ownership chain works in the same way as ownership chaining within a single database, except that an unbroken ownership chain requires that all the object owners are mapped to the same login account. If the source object in the source database and the target objects in the target databases are owned by the same login account, SQL Server does not check permissions on the target objects. Ownership chaining across databases is turned off by default. Microsoft recommends that you disable cross-database ownership chaining because it exposes you to some security risks.
community_validation: Official Microsoft SQL Assessment API rules - enterprise-grade reliability

displayName: HP Logical Processor issue
enhancement_notes: Extracted from official Microsoft DefaultRuleset.csv - comprehensive enterprise ruleset
sql_query: -- MANUAL SQL DEVELOPMENT NEEDED:  Microsoft Assessment API rule - SQL extraction requires API evaluation
message: Review: HP Logical Processor issue
expected_result: Rule evaluation via Microsoft SQL Assessment API
description: HP ProLiant Gen9 servers configured with Intel Xeon E5 2600 v3 processors can be configured to support processor configurations that exceed 64 logical processors. Windows Operating Systems divide the logical processors into groups (Kgroups). On HP ProLiant Gen9 servers, the System Firmware, by default, reports the maximum number of possible logical processors to reflect the highest core count processor available. The result is that Windows Operating Systems will group the available logical processors into more than one Kgroup in a two-socket populated configuration. Applications that rely on older kernel APIs for affnitizing threads inside a process to a specific logical processor will only be able to address one Kgroup. The result is that the process will run on fewer logical processors than are presented to the OS for use. Workloads that utilize multiple processes (in lieu of a single process with multiple threads), or are leveraging the APIs that allow beyond single Kgroup addressing, will have full access to all the logical processors presented to the OS for use.
community_validation: Official Microsoft SQL Assessment API rules - enterprise-grade reliability

sql_query: -- MANUAL SQL DEVELOPMENT NEEDED:  Microsoft Assessment API rule - SQL extraction requires API evaluation
message: Review: Lightweight pooling option disabled
expected_result: Rule evaluation via Microsoft SQL Assessment API
description: The 'lightweight pooling' option is used to configure SQL Server to use simplified flows (or 'fibers'). Fiber mode is intended for certain situations in which the context switching of the UMS workers are the critical bottleneck in performance. Because this is rare, fiber mode rarely enhances performance or scalability on the typical system. Improved context switching in Microsoft Windows Server 2003 has also reduced the need for fiber mode. We do not recommend that you use fiber mode scheduling for routine operation. This is because it can decrease performance by inhibiting the regular benefits of context switching, and because some components of SQL Server that use Thread Local Storage (TLS) or thread-owned objects, such as mutexes (a type of Win32 kernel object), cannot function correctly in fiber mode.

message: Review: NTFS block size in volumes with database files <> 64KB
expected_result: Rule evaluation via Microsoft SQL Assessment API
description: A drive can be formatted with different sizes ranging from 512 bytes to 64K sizes with the default being 4KB (4096 bytes). This setting is also called as “Bytes Per Cluster”. The atomic unit of storage in SQL Server is a page which is 8KB in size. Extents are groups of eight 8 KB pages that are physically contiguous to each other for a total of 64 KB. SQL Server uses extents to store data. Hence, on a SQL Server machine the NTFS Allocation unit size hosting SQL database files (Including tempdb files) should be 64 KB.
community_validation: Official Microsoft SQL Assessment API rules - enterprise-grade reliability

enhancement_notes: Extracted from official Microsoft DefaultRuleset.csv - comprehensive enterprise ruleset
sql_query: -- MANUAL SQL DEVELOPMENT NEEDED:  Microsoft Assessment API rule - SQL extraction requires API evaluation
message: Review: Memory paged out
expected_result: Rule evaluation via Microsoft SQL Assessment API
description: Microsoft SQL Server performs dynamic memory management based on the memory requirements of the current load and activities on the system. On Windows, SQL Server can use the memory notification mechanisms that are provided by the QueryMemoryResourceNotification Windows API. Based on this information from the QueryMemoryResourceNotification Windows API or from the memory calculation, SQL Server responds to the current memory situation on a specific system. This provides the following benefits: 1. The system does not page out the working set of the SQL Server process; 2. The necessary database pages are available in memory to reduce physical I/O needs. For more information, see the "Dynamic memory management" topic and the "Server memory options" topic in SQL Server Books Online.

message: Review: Power plan is High Performance
expected_result: Rule evaluation via Microsoft SQL Assessment API
description: In some cases you may experience degraded overall performance on a Windows Server 2008 R2 or later machine when running with the default (Balanced) power plan. The issue may occur irrespective of platform and may be exhibited on both native and virtual environments. The degraded performance may increase the average response time for some tasks and cause performance issues with CPU-intensive applications. Please note that you may not notice performance issues while performing simple operations. However, applications or scripts that intensively use resources (primarily processor and memory) may exhibit the problem. To work around the performance degradation issue, you can switch to the High Performance power plan.  However, this will disable dynamic performance scaling on the platform. Depending on the environment, if the platform is always under a heavy load, then this is a viable solution.

message: Review: Remote admin connections enabled on cluster (DAC)
expected_result: Rule evaluation via Microsoft SQL Assessment API
description: SQL Server provides a dedicated administrator connection (DAC). The DAC lets an administrator access a running server to execute diagnostic functions or Transact-SQL statements, or to troubleshoot problems on the server, even when the server is locked or running in an abnormal state and not responding to a SQL Server Database Engine connection. On cluster configurations, the DAC will be off by default. Users can execute the remote admin connection option of sp_configure to enable the DAC listener to access a remote connection. If SQL Server is unresponsive and the DAC listener is not enabled, you might have to restart SQL Server to connect with the DAC. Therefore, we recommend that you enable the remote admin connections configuration option on clustered systems.

description: The 'cost threshold for parallelism' option specifies the threshold at which SQL Server creates and runs parallel plans for queries. SQL Server creates and runs a parallel plan for a query only when the estimated cost to run a serial plan for the same query is higher than the value set in the cost threshold for parallelism. The cost refers to an estimated cost required to run the serial plan on a specific hardware configuration and is not a unit of time. The 'cost threshold for parallelism' option can be set to any value from 0 through 32767. The default value is 5.

displayName: 'Auto-Create Statistics' option should be on
enhancement_notes: Added from comprehensive Microsoft VA extraction
sql_query: -- MANUAL SQL DEVELOPMENT NEEDED:  Microsoft Assessment API rule - SQL extraction requires API evaluation
message: Review: 'Auto-Create Statistics' option should be on
expected_result: Rule evaluation via Microsoft SQL Assessment API
description: The Query Optimizer determines whether an index is useful for a specific query by evaluating the stored statistics. If the statistics become out of date and significant changes have occurred against the underlying data, this can result in less than optimal query performance. In most cases, it's best to let SQL Server maintain the statistics. If you turn 'Auto Create Stats' and 'Auto Update Stats' off, then it is up to you to keep the statistics up-to-date somehow. Failure to do so will lead to poor query performance. Most applications should have these options ON. When the Auto Create statistics setting is ON, the Query Optimizer creates statistics on one or more columns of a table or an indexed view, as necessary, to improve query plans and query performance.
